1: The appeal to "those who know the law" signals that Paul is now addressing the Jewish members of the congregation (and Gentile God-fearers familiar with Torah) specifically. This is a live rhetorical move: the people being addressed have standing to evaluate the legal argument that follows. The principle that "the law has authority over a person only as long as he lives" was recognized in both Jewish and Roman law: death dissolved legal obligations. Roman law held that death ended contractual and marital bonds; Jewish law similarly understood death as dissolving the obligations of the covenant member to Torah.
2: The married-woman analogy was drawn from a legal reality both Jewish and Roman audiences would have known, though the specifics differed. Under Jewish law, the husband controlled the right of divorce (the bill of divorcement, gēt), with the Shammai-Hillel controversy over legitimate grounds being a well-known debate at the time Paul wrote. Under Roman law, either party could terminate the marriage. In both systems, the wife was legally bound to the husband while he lived. The point of the analogy is the absolute nature of the bond: it cannot be dissolved by the wife's choice alone while the husband lives.
3: The language of "adulteress" (moichalis) in the analogy is heavy with covenantal freight: throughout the OT prophets, Israel's covenant infidelity to God was described as adultery (Hos 2; Jeremiah 3:1-10; Ezek 16). The marital metaphor for the covenant — God as husband, Israel as wife — was deeply embedded in Jewish theological language. Paul's use of the adultery language therefore resonates beyond mere legal illustration: for Jewish readers, the image of a woman bound to one husband by law and able to legitimately join another only through the first husband's death carries unmistakable covenantal resonance.
4: The transfer from the law to Christ — dying to the law "through the body of Christ" — uses the physical reality of Christ's death as the ground of the legal transfer. The crucified body of Christ is the specific mechanism: the believer is united to Christ in his death (Romans 6), and it is in that death that the law's claim is discharged. The purpose is explicitly fruitfulness: "in order that we might bear fruit for God." The marriage metaphor extends: the new union with the risen Christ is productive, whereas the old relationship (under the law) produced "fruit for death" (v5). This is not the abolition of covenant relationship but its eschatological renewal.
5: The "sinful passions aroused by the law" describes the paradox of moral prohibition: the prohibition can intensify the desire it forbids. This was recognized in the ancient world both in Jewish theology and in Hellenistic philosophy. The Stoics discussed the capacity of prohibition to create fascination with what is forbidden. In the Genesis narrative, the prohibition of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil is what the serpent exploits — the existence of the commandment provides the leverage for temptation (Genesis 3:1-6). Paul will make this Genesis connection explicit in vv7-11.
6: The contrast between "the new way of the Spirit" and "the old way of the written code" (gramma, letter) echoes the new covenant promise of Jeremiah 31:31-34: "I will put my law in their minds and write it on their hearts." The old covenant was a written external code; the new covenant involves the Spirit writing the law internally. Paul had developed this contrast in 2 Corinthians 3:6 ("the letter kills, but the Spirit gives life"), also drawing on the Jeremiah text. The congregation at Rome would have been formed in this early Christian understanding of the Spirit as the new-covenant provision that the Torah anticipated but could not itself supply.
7: The example of coveting (epithymia) from the tenth commandment (Exodus 20:17; Deuteronomy 5:21) was not chosen at random. The tenth commandment is unique among the Ten: it prohibits an internal state (desiring) rather than an external act. Jewish teachers debated its scope and application: what exactly constitutes coveting? The Mishnah (Bava Metzia Romans 5:11) discusses cases of coveting and restitution. The commandment about coveting is precisely the one that reveals the inner dimension of sin — the law reaches inside the self, not just to regulate external behavior, and the internal reach is what Paul focuses on.
8: The image of sin "seizing the opportunity afforded by the commandment" to "produce every kind of coveting" may echo the Eden narrative: the serpent exploited the prohibition of the fruit to produce the desire for it. The word aphormē (opportunity, base of operations, bridgehead) is a military term — the base from which an attack is launched. Sin uses the commandment as its tactical position: the existence of "you shall not" provides the springboard for "I want." This is not a criticism of the commandment but an observation about sin's perverse ingenuity.
9: "Once I was alive apart from the law" is the anthropological baseline: before the law entered, there was a kind of pre-moral life — a condition Paul associates with childhood (when the child is not yet under full covenantal obligation) or, more likely, with Adam before the commandment was given (Genesis 2:16-17). The "commandment came" and "sin sprang to life" recapitulates the Eden sequence: God gave the commandment; the serpent activated it; death followed. Paul's "I" in this section is the representative human whose story is Adam's story.
10: The commandment "intended to bring life" but actually "bringing death" reflects the Torah's double-edged character in Second Temple thought. Deuteronomy 30:15-20 presents Torah observance as the path to life and blessing: "I set before you today life and prosperity, death and destruction... choose life." The law was designed as the life-path for the covenant people. But sin's entry means the law's conditions for life cannot be met — the very path that would lead to life, because it is a law, leads to judgment when the conditions are violated. The law is not the problem; sin, which corrupts the person who approaches the law, is the problem.
11: Sin "deceiving" through the commandment is the most explicit echo of Eden in this passage: the serpent deceived Eve through the prohibition (Genesis 3:13; 2 Corinthians 11:3; 1 Timothy 2:14 use the same vocabulary). The "I" who was deceived by sin-through-the-commandment is therefore reading Adam's history as his own. The personification of sin as an active agent — seizing, deceiving, producing, killing — was a recognized Jewish mode of speech; the evil inclination (yetzer hara) in rabbinic thought was similarly personified as an internal adversary that uses legitimate desires and even religious duty as leverage.
12: The formal declaration — "the law is holy, and the commandment is holy, righteous, and good" — is Paul's careful repudiation of any Marcionite misreading before it could arise. The three adjectives ("holy, righteous, good") are all positive theological terms: the law shares the character of the God who gave it. The problem diagnosed in vv7-11 is not a defect in the law but sin's exploitation of the law. Jewish readers would have expected and required this affirmation; Paul's argument about the law's limitations is not a denigration of Torah but a diagnosis of what sin has done with and through it.
13: The rhetorical question — "did that which is good become death to me?" — and its emphatic denial set up the next stage of the argument: sin is the culprit, not the law. The purpose of sin's lethal work through the good commandment is diagnostic: it reveals sin in its full character (hina genētai kath' hyperbolēn hamartōlos hē hamartia, "so that sin might become utterly sinful"). The law becomes a diagnostic instrument: when you know the good commandment and still violate it, the violation is unambiguously sinful, stripped of any defense of ignorance. The commandment's goodness makes sin's badness maximally visible.
14: The identification of the law as "spiritual" (pneumatikos) and the "I" as "unspiritual" (sarkinos, fleshly, made of flesh) introduces the flesh-spirit contrast that Paul uses throughout his letters. Sarx (flesh) in Paul does not mean the physical body per se but the human person insofar as she is oriented toward her own resources rather than toward God — the self curved in on itself (homo incurvatus in se, in Luther's later formulation). The "sold as a slave to sin" language completes the picture: the fleshly person is in bondage, not free. The debate among interpreters — whether the "I" here is pre-Christian, Christian, or Adam/Israel — was already alive in the Patristic period; the passage's ambiguity may be deliberate.
15: The description of the divided will — "what I want to do I do not do, but what I hate I do" — has a famous Greek parallel in Ovid's Metamorphoses (Romans 7:20-21), written approximately two generations before Paul: video meliora proboque, deteriora sequor ("I see and approve the better things, but follow the worse"). Medea speaks these words as she is drawn toward what she knows to be wrong. The akrasia problem (weakness of will, incontinence) was a standard topic of ancient moral philosophy from Aristotle onward. Paul is engaging this recognized philosophical problem but reframing it: the cause is not weakness of character but slavery to sin.
16: The admission that the "I" agrees with the law's goodness even while unable to obey it is a crucial move: the will is not simply opposed to the law but divided. Something in the person recognizes the law's rightness and assents to it — Paul will call this the "inner person" (v22) and the "mind" (v23, 25). This preserved capacity for moral recognition — the conscience that acknowledges the law's goodness while the will fails — is what separates the divided person of Romans 7 from the completely conscience-dead state Paul describes in Romans 1:18-32. The law has not been entirely lost; it has been rendered practically ineffective by sin.
17: The statement "it is no longer I who do it, but sin living in me" is not a denial of moral responsibility but a description of the mechanism of moral failure. In Jewish thought, the yetzer hara (evil inclination) was an internal power that could not be identified simply with the self — it was conceived as a malevolent resident within the person that had to be resisted. Paul's "sin living in me" has the same structure: sin is not the self but an alien power that has taken up residence. The practical implication is that self-improvement cannot solve the problem; something stronger than self must expel the occupying power.
18: The acknowledgment that "good itself does not dwell in me, that is, in my sinful nature" (sarx) targets the Greco-Roman optimism about human moral potential. Stoic philosophy taught that the rational faculty (logos) within the human person, properly cultivated, was sufficient for virtue. Platonic thought localized the good in the rational soul, corrupted by the body. Paul's diagnosis is more radical: the problem is not the body per se but the whole person oriented toward self-sovereignty rather than God. The desire to do good exists (v18b) — the Stoics were right that human beings have moral aspirations — but the execution is broken.
19: The repetition of the paradox — "the good I want to do I do not do; the evil I do not want to do, I keep on doing" — with its slight variation from v15 intensifies the analysis. The phrase "I keep on doing" (touto prassō, present active — ongoing action) emphasizes the chronic, habitual nature of the failure rather than isolated lapses. This is not the occasional slip of the basically righteous person but the structural condition of the person living in the flesh without the Spirit's liberation. The accumulated weight of the repeated verb conveys the exhausted recognition of a pattern the person cannot break.
20: The double appearance of "it is no longer I who do it but sin living in me" (here and in v17) creates a rhetorical frame around the central description of moral failure. The repetition is not redundant but structural: Paul is building a sustained argument that the divided-will experience is not merely psychological but theological — it reveals the reality of sin's occupying power. For first-century readers who understood the person as a unity of body and soul (rather than a Platonic body-prison for the soul), the claim that sin could be a distinct resident power within the person was a specifically theological claim, not a common-sense observation.
21: "This law at work" — that good is desired but evil is present — describes what Paul finds (heuriskō) by empirical observation of his own experience and the human condition generally. The word nomos here means something like "a principle" or "rule" — Paul observes a pattern as regular as a law of nature. This is the phenomenon that requires explanation: why does the person who wills the good reliably produce evil? Neither Stoic self-discipline nor Jewish Torah-keeping has solved it. The solution will not come from within the person, as v24's cry of desperation makes clear.
22: The "inner person" (esō anthrōpos) who "delights in God's law" resonates with the Jewish tradition of the Torah as delight and treasure: Ps 119 is the extended lyric of the Torah-lover whose deepest pleasure is in God's commands. The Shema's command to love God "with all your heart, soul, and strength" (Deuteronomy 6:5) points to this interior orientation as the covenantal ideal. Paul is claiming that even the person living in the flesh, unable to obey, may have an inner layer of genuine Torah-delight — which makes the outer failure all the more agonizing rather than excusing it.
23: The "law of my mind" versus the "law of sin at work within my members" presents the divided person as a contested territory, with two rival laws (nomos used in the sense of "governing principle") fighting for control. This is a militarized image: sin is at war, making the person a prisoner of war. The concept of internal moral conflict as warfare has roots in both Jewish and Hellenistic thought. The Dead Sea Scrolls' War Scroll describes a cosmic conflict that has its counterpart in the inner life; the Stoic tradition spoke of the war of passions against reason. Paul's version is theologically specific: the enemy within is sin, not the body or the passions per se.
24: The cry "What a wretched man I am! Who will rescue me?" (tis me rhysetai) is an outcry of despair at the situation described in vv14-23, not a loss of faith. The cry is structured as a rhetorical question that expects and receives an answer: "Thanks be to God, through Jesus Christ our Lord!" The term talaipōros (wretched, miserable) is used in the LXX for Israel under divine judgment and in the prophets for those in desperate need of rescue. The question "who?" looks beyond human resources — no amount of self-discipline, Torah-observance, or philosophical training has solved the problem diagnosed in this chapter.
25: The doxological answer — "Thanks be to God, through Jesus Christ our Lord!" — is the same form of spontaneous praise that Paul uses elsewhere when the weight of grace becomes overwhelming (2 Corinthians 9:15; 1 Corinthians 15:57). The summary that follows ("in my mind I am a slave to God's law, but in my sinful nature a slave to the law of sin") is the concise restatement of the divided-will situation of the whole chapter — but now the question "who will rescue?" has been answered. The rescue is not yet fully experienced in the flesh, which is why Romans 8's pneumatology follows immediately: the Spirit is the power that delivers what Romans 7's "I" could not achieve alone.
